source.title: ANF03. Latin Christianity: Its Founder, Tertullian

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But we know that prophecy expressed itself by things no less than by words.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: By words, and also by deeds, is the resurrection foretold.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: When Moses puts his hand into his bosom, and then draws it out again dead, and again puts his hand into his bosom, and plucks it out living, does not this apply as a presage to all mankind?—inasmuch as those three signs denoted the threefold power of God: when it shall, first, in the appointed order, subdue to man the old serpent, the devil, however formidable; then, secondly, draw forth the flesh from the bosom of death; and then, at last, shall pursue all blood (shed) in judgment.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: On this subject we read in the writings of the same prophet, (how that) God says: “For your blood of your lives will I require of all wild beasts; and I will require it of the hand of man, and of his brother’s hand.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now nothing is required except that which is demanded back again, and nothing is thus demanded except that which is to be given up; and that will of course be given up, which shall be demanded and required on the ground of vengeance.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: But indeed there cannot possibly be punishment of that which never had any existence.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Existence, however, it will have, when it is restored in order to be punished.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: To the flesh, therefore, applies everything which is declared respecting the blood, for without the flesh there cannot be blood.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: The flesh will be raised up in order that the blood may be punished.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: There are, again, some statements (of Scripture) so plainly made as to be free from all obscurity of allegory, and yet they strongly require their very simplicity to be interpreted.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: There is, for instance, that passage in Isaiah: “I will kill, and I will make alive.”

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Certainly His making alive is to take place after He has killed.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: As, therefore, it is by death that He kills, it is by the resurrection that He will make alive.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Now it is the flesh which is killed by death; the flesh, therefore, will be revived by the resurrection.

verses.book: On the Resurrection of the Flesh.
verses.text: Surely if killing means taking away life from the flesh, and its opposite, reviving, amounts to restoring life to the flesh, it must needs be that the flesh rise again, to which the life, which has been taken away by killing, has to be restored by vivification.
